items.q: What did Renaissance artists rediscover that medieval art lacked?
items.correct: Linear perspective and naturalistic representation of the human body.
items.why: Students assume medieval art was colourless. It was vivid but flat - the Renaissance breakthrough was mathematical PERSPECTIVE (Brunelleschi) and anatomical realism, not the introduction of colour.

items.correct: Removing material from a solid block to reveal the form (e.g., carving stone).
items.why: Students confuse 'subtractive' with arithmetic. In sculpture, additive = building up (clay, welding); subtractive = taking away (carving). Michelangelo described 'freeing' figures from the marble - quintessential subtractive thinking.

items.correct: An object's design should be determined by its purpose.
items.misconception: Decoration is the most important aspect of design.
items.random_wrong: Designers must follow strict government forms.
items.why: Students invert the meaning. Sullivan's modernist principle prioritises FUNCTION over ornament - the chair's shape should serve sitting, not the other way around. Postmodernism later challenged this.

items.q: Which technique uses crossing lines for tone?
items.why: Correct answer: Cross-hatching. “Stippling” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Dots. “Lines” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many primary colours are commonly used in pigment mixing?
items.why: The real figure is 3 colours. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
